---
title: Claude connectors, extensions, plugins, skills, and MCP are different layers
format: gfm
preset: georgian
preset_dir: presets/georgian
---

# Claude connectors, extensions, plugins, skills, and MCP are different layers

Spend ten minutes inside Anthropic's ecosystem and you can come away thinking the company has five names for the same thing. In one place the product talks about connectors, in another skills, in another plugins, and inside Claude Desktop the UI talks about extensions. The stack is not actually that messy. The terminology is just describing different layers.

## Why this vocabulary feels messier than it is

The common mistake is to treat every Claude add-on as a plugin. That shortcut sounds harmless, but it collapses protocol, integration, packaging, and reusable instructions into one blurry category.

## The product teaches different words in different places

That confusion is understandable because Anthropic spreads the vocabulary across multiple products and surfaces. The docs describe connectors as integrations built on MCP, Claude Desktop uses desktop extensions for local packaged capability, Claude Code and Cowork talk about plugins, and older DXT language still shows up next to newer MCPB terminology. If you are trying to explain the stack to a team, the labels start sounding interchangeable even when they are not.

## The clean model is protocol, integration, instruction, and package

The clean way to think about it is to separate what Claude can reach, what Claude knows, how a capability is packaged, and where it runs. Once you do that, the terms become much easier to use precisely: MCP is the protocol, connectors are integrations, skills are reusable instructions, desktop extensions package local Desktop capability, and plugins bundle multiple capabilities together.

## MCP is the protocol, not the add-on

[MCP](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/mcp) is the protocol layer, not the add-on you install. It is the open standard Anthropic introduced so Claude clients can talk to tools, resources, and prompts through a consistent interface. That is why phrases like "we added an MCP" are usually imprecise. What you added was a connector, a local extension, or some other package built on top of MCP.

## Connectors are the integration layer

Anthropic's [connectors overview](https://claude.com/docs/connectors/overview) uses `connector` for the integration layer that gives Claude access to external tools, data, and UI. If Claude can reach GitHub, Slack, Google Drive, or a custom remote service, `connector` is usually the right word. That term is useful because it is about what Claude can reach, not how the capability was packaged. In plain English: a connector is the thing that actually connects Claude to something else.

## Desktop extensions are the local packaging model

`Desktop extension` is narrower. Anthropic's support docs on [desktop and web connectors](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11725091-when-to-use-desktop-and-web-connectors) use it for local Claude Desktop capability: access to local files, installed applications, internal tools, and other machine-local resources. So a desktop extension is not the generic name for every Claude integration. It is the Claude Desktop packaging and installation model for local capability. If the capability is cloud-hosted and available across clients, Anthropic is more likely to describe it as a connector instead.

## Skills teach Claude how to work

Anthropic's [skills overview](https://claude.com/docs/skills/overview) makes the cleanest distinction in the stack: skills are task-specific procedures that Claude loads when relevant. A skill can include instructions, scripts, and supporting resources, but its job is to teach Claude how to do something, not to create a new connection to an external system. A deployment checklist, a brand-voice workflow, or a company-specific research process is a skill. Even if that workflow later uses a connector, the skill and the connector are still different things.

## Plugins bundle capabilities instead of naming every add-on

The term `plugin` matters because Anthropic uses it officially, but in a more specific way than most people do. The [plugins overview](https://claude.com/docs/plugins/overview) defines plugins as shareable capability packages that can bundle connectors, skills, slash commands, and sub-agents. That makes `plugin` a packaging word, not the universal label for anything that extends Claude. In Claude Code and Cowork, `plugin` is precise. In Claude Desktop conversations, people often use it as shorthand for connectors or extensions, and that is where a lot of the confusion starts.

## Why even careful users keep mixing these terms up

The terminology keeps collapsing because the categories sit next to each other in the product. Anthropic's [unified directory](https://support.claude.com/en/articles/14328846-browse-skills-connectors-and-plugins-in-one-directory) lets you browse skills, connectors, and plugins from one place, which is good product design but weak taxonomy teaching. The platform split adds another layer: Claude Desktop emphasizes extensions, Claude Code emphasizes plugins, and the docs describe connectors across products. Add the rename from DXT to [MCPB](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/mcpb), and it is easy to see why even technical users start flattening everything into one word.

## A simple map for using the right term

| If you mean... | Use this term | Why |
|---|---|---|
| The open standard underneath tool integrations | `MCP` | It is the protocol layer |
| A Claude integration to a web or cloud service | `connector` | It describes what Claude can reach |
| A local Claude Desktop installable for machine-local capability | `desktop extension` or `MCPB` | It describes the Desktop packaging model |
| Reusable instructions or workflow logic | `skill` | It describes what Claude knows how to do |
| A shareable bundle that combines several capabilities | `plugin` | It describes the package, not the protocol |

The shortcut that causes the most damage is calling all five of those things `plugins`. It sounds convenient, but it hides the setup model, the security model, and the product surface you are actually talking about.

## The label changes the expectation

This distinction matters because the wrong label creates the wrong expectation. If you call a connector a plugin, a teammate may assume there is a bundle or marketplace package involved. If you call a desktop extension an MCP, you lose the difference between the protocol and the local Claude Desktop installable. If you call a skill an integration, you imply that Claude gained access to a system when it really gained instructions. Precision here is not pedantry. It is how you avoid confusion about setup, permissions, portability, and where the capability actually runs.

## The stack makes sense once you sort the layer

The easiest way to keep the stack straight is to ask one question before you name anything: are you talking about the protocol, the connection, the instructions, the package, or the product surface? Once you sort the terms that way, Claude's ecosystem stops looking like a bag of synonyms and starts looking like a fairly normal stack with different layers doing different jobs.
